subject: Physics
q: An electron (mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) with initial velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is in an electric filed <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is initial de-Broglie wavelength of electron, its de-Broglie wave length at time <math><mi>t</mi></math> is given by:
question: An electron (mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) with initial velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is in an electric filed <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is initial de-Broglie wavelength of electron, its de-Broglie wave length at time <math><mi>t</mi></math> is given by:
solution:
Initial velocity of electron, 

v=v0i^+v0j^⇒u=v02+v02=v02

Initial de Broglie wavelength associated with electron, 

λ0=hp=hmv02

now,

v=u+at
Velocity as a function of time =v0i^+v0j^+eE0mtk^
so wavelength λ=hm2v02+e2E02m2t2
λ=λ01+e2E022m2v02t2

subject: Physics
q: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a carbon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn></math> atom has same kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>12</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a carbon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn></math> atom has same kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>12</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength is stated as λ=hp=h2mK, where, K is kinetic energy and m is mass.

Here, Kα=KC‐12.

Now, the ratio λαλC12=12m4m=3:1

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an α-particle are accelerated from rest by 2 V and 4 V potentials, respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is :
question: A proton and an α-particle are accelerated from rest by 2 V and 4 V potentials, respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is :
solution:
The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the proton is given by

λp=h2mpqpV   ...1

The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the alpha particle is given by

λα=h2mαqαV'   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

λpλα=mαmp×qαV'qpV   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

λpλα=41×2×41×2=4:1

subject: Physics
q: The radiation pressure exerted by a 450 W light source on a perfectly reflecting surface placed at 2 m away from it, is :
question: The radiation pressure exerted by a 450 W light source on a perfectly reflecting surface placed at 2 m away from it, is :
solution:
$P_{\mathrm{rad}}=\frac{2 I}{\mathrm{C}}$
Where $\mathrm{I}=$ intensity at surface
$\mathrm{C}=$ Speed of light
$\begin{aligned}
& I=\frac{\text { Power }}{\text { Area }}=\frac{450}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2} \\ & =\frac{450}{4 \pi \times 4}=\frac{450}{16 \pi} \\ & P_{\mathrm{rad}}=\frac{2 \times 450}{16 \pi \times 3 \times 10^8}=\frac{150}{8 \pi \times 10^8}
\end{aligned}$
$=5.97 \times 10^{-8} \approx 6 \times 10^{-8}$ Pascals

subject: Physics
q: A metallic surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the same surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>λ</mi></math>, the stopping potential becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. The threshold wavelength for this metallic surface will be
question: A metallic surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the same surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>λ</mi></math>, the stopping potential becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. The threshold wavelength for this metallic surface will be
solution:
Let the threshold frequency be λ0.

By the equation of photoelectric effect, for wavelength λ,

eV0=hc1λ-1λ0   ...(i)

For the wavelength 2λ,

eV04=hc12λ-1λ0   ...(ii)

Dividing (i) by (ii)

4=hc(λ0-λλλ0)hcλ0-2λ2λλ0⇒4=2(λ0-λ)λ0-2λ⇒2λ0=6λ⇒λ0=3λ

subject: Physics
q:
In a photoelectric experiment, ultraviolet light of wavelength 280 nm is used with lithium cathode having work function ϕ=2.5 eV. If the wavelength of incident light is switched to 400 nm, find out the change in the stopping potential.

h=6.63×10-34 J s, c=3×108 m s-1
question:
In a photoelectric experiment, ultraviolet light of wavelength 280 nm is used with lithium cathode having work function ϕ=2.5 eV. If the wavelength of incident light is switched to 400 nm, find out the change in the stopping potential.

h=6.63×10-34 J s, c=3×108 m s-1
solution:
From the photoelectric equation,

eV0=hcλ−ϕ

Using this equation for the given two cases,

eV1=hc280 nm−ϕ

eV2=hc400 nm−ϕ

On subtracting,

eV1−V2=1240120280×400                   =1.3 eV

⇒V1−V2=1.3 V

q: Sodium and copper have work functions $2.3 \mathrm{eV}$ and $4.5 \mathrm{eV}$ respectively. Then the ratio of the wave lengths is nearest to
question: Sodium and copper have work functions $2.3 \mathrm{eV}$ and $4.5 \mathrm{eV}$ respectively. Then the ratio of the wave lengths is nearest to
solution: $\mathrm{hc} / \lambda_0=\mathrm{W}_0 ; \frac{\left(\lambda_0\right)_1}{\left(\lambda_0\right)_2}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{W}_0\right)_2}{\left(\mathrm{~W}_0\right)_1}=\frac{4.5}{2.3}=2: 1$

subject: Physics
q: A small mirror of mass $m$ is suspended by a massless thread of length $l$. Then the small angle through which the thread will be deflected when a short pulse of laser of energy $E$ falls normal on the mirror ( $\mathrm{c}=$ speed of light in vacuum and $\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity)
question: A small mirror of mass $m$ is suspended by a massless thread of length $l$. Then the small angle through which the thread will be deflected when a short pulse of laser of energy $E$ falls normal on the mirror ( $\mathrm{c}=$ speed of light in vacuum and $\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
Force due to beam assuming complete reflection $\mathrm{F}=\frac{2 \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{C}} \frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dt}} ; \mathrm{P}$ is power
So change in momentum of mirror.
$\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{~V}-0)=\int \mathrm{Fdt}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{C}} \int \mathrm{dE}=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{C}}$
Now using work energy theorem ...(1)
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}=\Delta \mathrm{k} \\ & -\mathrm{mg} \ell(1-\cos \theta)=0-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2 \\ & \mathrm{~g} \ell\left(2 \sin ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{2}
\end{aligned}$
as $\theta$ is small
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{g} \ell 2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{4 \mathrm{E}^2}{\mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{c}^2} \\ & \mathrm{~g} \ell \theta^2=\frac{4 \mathrm{E}^2}{\mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{c}^2} \\ & \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{mc} \sqrt{\mathrm{g} \ell}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of wavelengths of proton and deuteron accelerated by potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. Then, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> will be
question: The ratio of wavelengths of proton and deuteron accelerated by potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. Then, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> will be
solution:
Kinetic energy gained by a charged particle accelerated by a potential V is qV

KE=qV

Using relation between momentum and kinetic energy

⇒p22 m=qV⇒p=2mqV

We know that momentum of charged particle is p=hλ.

Thus, λ=h2mqV

Now, λpλd=mdVdmpVp

⇒12=2VdVp⇒VpVd=4

q: The variation of stopping potential V0 as a function of the frequency (ν) of the incident light for a metal is shown in figure. The work function of the surface is
question: The variation of stopping potential V0 as a function of the frequency (ν) of the incident light for a metal is shown in figure. The work function of the surface is
solution:
The relation between the stopping potential and the frequency of the incident light is given by

eV0=hν-ϕ   ...1

When V0 = 0,

ϕ=hν   ...2

Substitute the value of the frequency corresponding to zero stopping potential into equation (2) to calculate the required work function of the material.

ϕ=6.626×10-34 J·s×5×1014 Hz1.6×10-19 J/eV=6.626×516≈2.07eV

subject: Physics
q: An electron is travelling with a velocity $v$ in free space and when it enters a medium, its velocity is reduced by $20\%$. The de Broglie wavelength of electron in the medium is $\alpha\lambda_0$, where $\lambda_0$ is its de Broglie wavelength in free space. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
question: An electron is travelling with a velocity $v$ in free space and when it enters a medium, its velocity is reduced by $20\%$. The de Broglie wavelength of electron in the medium is $\alpha\lambda_0$, where $\lambda_0$ is its de Broglie wavelength in free space. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of an electron in free space is given by $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{mv}$.

When the electron enters the medium, its velocity is reduced by $20\%$. The new velocity is $v' = v - 0.20v = 0.80v$.

The de Broglie wavelength in the medium is $\lambda' = \dfrac{h}{mv'}$.

Substituting $v' = 0.80v$, we get $\lambda' = \dfrac{h}{m(0.80v)} = \dfrac{1}{0.80} \left(\dfrac{h}{mv}\right)$.

Since \dfrac{1}{0.80} = \dfrac{100}{80} = 1.25$, we have $\lambda' = 1.25\lambda_0$.

Comparing this with $\lambda' = \alpha\lambda_0$, we get $\alpha = 1.25$.

Answer: $1.25$

What should nearly be the ratio of their wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>?</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00727</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00055</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:

q: A proton moving with one tenth of velocity of light has a certain de Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. An alpha particle having certain kinetic energy has the same de-Brogle wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The ratio of kinetic energy of proton and that of alpha particle is :
question: A proton moving with one tenth of velocity of light has a certain de Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. An alpha particle having certain kinetic energy has the same de-Brogle wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The ratio of kinetic energy of proton and that of alpha particle is :
solution:
As we know, de Broglie wavelength is given by,

λ=hp

Now kinetic energy can be written as,

K=12mv2=p22m=h22mλ2

Therefore,

K∝1m

Hence, required ratio

KpKα=mαmp=4⇒mα:mp=4:1

question:

Given E (in  eV ) =1237λ(in nm)
solution:
From Einstein’s photoelectric equation,
K.Emax=hcλ-hcλ0 (here, λ0= threshold wavelength)
=1237260-1237380
=1237380-260380×260

subject: Physics
q:
A monochromatic light is incident on a metallic plate having work function $\phi$. An electron, emitted normally to the plate from a point $A$ with maximum kinetic energy, enters a constant magnetic field, perpendicular to the initial velocity of electron. The electron passes through a curve and hits back the plate at a point $B$. The distance between A and B is :
(Given : The magnitude of charge of an electron is e and mass is $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{h}$ is Planck's constant and c is velocity of light. Take the magnetic field exists throughout the path of electron)
question:
A monochromatic light is incident on a metallic plate having work function $\phi$. An electron, emitted normally to the plate from a point $A$ with maximum kinetic energy, enters a constant magnetic field, perpendicular to the initial velocity of electron. The electron passes through a curve and hits back the plate at a point $B$. The distance between A and B is :
(Given : The magnitude of charge of an electron is e and mass is $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{h}$ is Planck's constant and c is velocity of light. Take the magnetic field exists throughout the path of electron)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{KE}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi \\ & \mathrm{p}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}_{\max }} \\ & \mathrm{p}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi\right)} \\ & \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{R} \\ & =2\left[\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{qB}}\right]\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi\right)}}{\mathrm{eB}}=\frac{\sqrt{8 \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi\right)}}{\mathrm{eB}}$

subject: Physics
q: A small object at rest, absorbs a light pulse of power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mW</mi></math> and duration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ns</mi></math>. Assuming speed of light as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The momentum of the object becomes equal to :
question: A small object at rest, absorbs a light pulse of power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mW</mi></math> and duration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ns</mi></math>. Assuming speed of light as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The momentum of the object becomes equal to :
solution:
Momentum p=EnergyESpeed of lightc

=Power×timec

=20×10-3W300×10-9s3×108 m s-1

=2×10-17 kg m s-1

As momentum is conserved, therefore momentum of object will be 2×10-17 kg m s-1.

subject: Physics
q:
The ratio of de Broglie wavelength of a deutron with kinetic energy $E$ to that of an alpha particle with kinetic energy $2 E$, is $n: 1$. The value of $n$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Assume mass of proton $=$ mass of neutron) :
question:
The ratio of de Broglie wavelength of a deutron with kinetic energy $E$ to that of an alpha particle with kinetic energy $2 E$, is $n: 1$. The value of $n$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Assume mass of proton $=$ mass of neutron) :
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}}$.
For a deuteron (mass $m_d = 2m_p$) with energy $E$: $\lambda_d = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 2m_p \cdot E}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{4m_p E}}$
For an alpha particle (mass $m_\alpha = 4m_p$) with energy $2E$: $\lambda_\alpha = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 4m_p \cdot 2E}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{16m_p E}}$
The ratio: $\frac{\lambda_d}{\lambda_\alpha} = \frac{\sqrt{16m_p E}}{\sqrt{4m_p E}} = \sqrt{4} = 2$

subject: Physics
q:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).
Question: In an experiment, electrons are made to pass through a narrow slit of width 'd' comparable to their de Broglie wavelength. They are detected on a screen at a distance ' $D$ ' from the slit (see figure).
Which of the following graph can be expected to represent the number of electrons ' $N$ ' detected as a function of the detector position ' $y$ ' $(y=0$ corresponds to the middle of the slit)?
question:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).
Question: In an experiment, electrons are made to pass through a narrow slit of width 'd' comparable to their de Broglie wavelength. They are detected on a screen at a distance ' $D$ ' from the slit (see figure).
Which of the following graph can be expected to represent the number of electrons ' $N$ ' detected as a function of the detector position ' $y$ ' $(y=0$ corresponds to the middle of the slit)?
solution: Diffraction pattern will be wider than the slit.

subject: Physics
q: A proton, an electron and an alpha particle have the same energies. Their de-Broglie wavelengths will be compared as :
question: A proton, an electron and an alpha particle have the same energies. Their de-Broglie wavelengths will be compared as :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lambda_{\mathrm{DB}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mk}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{DB}} \alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{a}} < \lambda_{\mathrm{p}} < \lambda_{\mathrm{e}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The threshold frequency of metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. When the light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is incident on the metal plate, the maximum velocity of photoelectron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When the frequency of incident radiation is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. the maximum velocity of photoelectrons emitted is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is:
question: The threshold frequency of metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. When the light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is incident on the metal plate, the maximum velocity of photoelectron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When the frequency of incident radiation is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. the maximum velocity of photoelectrons emitted is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is:
solution:
Maximum kinetic energy is related to the incident frequency f and the threshold frequency f0 as:

Kmax=hf-hf0

When, f=2f0

12mv12=2hf0-hf0=hf0     ...1

When, f=5f0

12mv22=5hf0-hf0=4hf0    ...2

Hence, the ratio, v1v2=12

question: De-Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated by a voltage of <math> <mrow> <mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>V</mtext></mrow> </math> is close to <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mn>C</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>9.1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mn>k</mn><mn>g</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mn>J </mn><mn>s</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength λ in given by  

λ=hmv=h2meV

and for electron λ=12.27 A°V

=12.27 A°50

=1.7 A°

The work function of the metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy of the incident photons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi></math> and only <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of it produces photo electrons. The number of emitted photo electron  per second and their maximum energy, respectively, will be: <math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi>J</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

The work function of the metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy of the incident photons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi></math> and only <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of it produces photo electrons. The number of emitted photo electron  per second and their maximum energy, respectively, will be: <math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi>J</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Einstein's photoelectric equation
Maximum KE=hCλ-ϕ

=10-5

=5 eV

Intensity I=NphCλtA⇒Npt=IAhCλ

and number of electrons per second. =Npt×10%

=IAhCλ×110=16×10-3×10-410×1.6×10-19×10

=1011 per sec

q: An electron (mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>) with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is moving in an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is constant. If at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, de-Broglie wavelength is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math>, then its de-Broglie wavelength after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given by
question: An electron (mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>) with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is moving in an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is constant. If at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, de-Broglie wavelength is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math>, then its de-Broglie wavelength after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given by
solution:
Initial de-Broglie wavelength of electron,

λ0=hmv0 ....(i)

Force on electron in electric field,

F=-eE=-e-E0i^=eE0i^

Acceleration of electron, a=Fm=eE0i^m
Velocity of electron after time t,

v=v0i^+eE0i^mt=v0+eE0mti^=v01+eE0mv0ti^

de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron at time t is

λ=hmv

⇒λ=hmv01+eE0mv0t=λ01+eE0mv0t

subject: Physics
q: A particle having electric charge $3 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ and mass $6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ is accelerated by applying an electric potential of 1.21 V. Wavelength of the matter wave associated with the particle is $\alpha \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ - (Take Planck's constant $=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$)
question: A particle having electric charge $3 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ and mass $6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ is accelerated by applying an electric potential of 1.21 V. Wavelength of the matter wave associated with the particle is $\alpha \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ - (Take Planck's constant $=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$)
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$ where $v$ is found from energy conservation.
From $qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$: $v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.21}{6 \times 10^{-27}}} = \sqrt{1.21 \times 10^8} = 1.1 \times 10^4$ m/s.
Therefore: $\lambda = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{6 \times 10^{-27} \times 1.1 \times 10^4} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{6.6 \times 10^{-23}} = 10^{-11}$ m = $10 \times 10^{-12}$ m.
Thus $\alpha = 10$.
